text: The surgeon applies upward traction on the stomach using a single side arm to open the gastrocolic ligament. The tumor is identified at the proximal pancreatic body, and adhesions are detached. Dissection of the inferior border of the pancreatic neck exposes the superior mesenteric vein, splenic vein, and portal vein confluence. Small tributaries of the splenic vessels are carefully isolated and resected to preserve the distal pancreas.

The surgeon achieves vascular control using two articulating instruments, creating a complete window at the pancreatic neck. A nylon tape is introduced to facilitate the division of the pancreas, which is then divided using an endo-GIA stapler. The distal pancreas is retracted with an elastic rubber band to prevent laceration of the soft pancreatic tissue during retraction.

The surgeon dissects along the splenic vessels, determining the second resection line. Sutures are placed on the inferior and superior edges of the distal pancreatic stump to facilitate anastomosis. The segment of the pancreas, including the tumor, is resected using ultrasonic shears. The deep pancreatic parenchyma is approached, and the pancreatic tissue is resected using the active jaw's vibration to prevent coagulation of the pancreatic duct. The pancreatic duct is identified and cut with laparoscopic scissors by the assistant surgeon.

The surgeon controls small bleeders by suturing with Proline-Six-O. The proximal jejunum is resected using an endo-GIA after trimming the mesentery. The distal jejunal limb is pulled up through the mesocolon and positioned alongside the distal pancreas. A short silicon catheter is inserted into the pancreatic duct, and a pancreatic jejunostomy is performed.

The surgeon performs posterior outer sutures on the distal body of the pancreas, preparing for anastomosis. The surgical plane shifts away from the pancreatic neck, highlighting the challenges of using non-articulating laparoscopic instruments. The anastomosis is executed in a duct-to-mucosa manner with interrupted sutures.

The surgeon completes the pancreaticojejunal anastomosis with anterior outer sutures. The mesocolon defect is secured with the jejunal limb to prevent internal hernia. An intracorporeal jejunojejunostomy is performed using VLOC-III-O suture. The procedure concludes with the placement of a two-armed drain tube around the pancreatic jejunostomy site.
